questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B with 86M parameters. Note: ViT-L (307M) appears in the paper as a comparison baseline, not as a DeiT model variant.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed in the evidence is ViT-L, with 307M parameters. If you mean the largest DeiT variant in this paper, DeiT-B★ is 87M parameters.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention in non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design gives linear computational complexity with respect to image size. Relative position bias is another architectural choice highlighted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper uses DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer setup based on self-distillation without labels. A student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening to prevent collapse; the method is applied to ViTs (especially small-patch ViTs) and also works on convnets.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The reported ImageNet top-1 accuracies are highly inconsistent across the corpus, ranging from 1.3 to 91.0 (spread 89.7, stddev 8.69). There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower top results such as PaLI-17B at 90.9 and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51, indicating multiple different claimed best values rather than a single consistent result.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: Not enough data on ADE20K/mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU) to compare.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The reported ADE20K mIoU values are not consistent: across 97 results they range from 26.6 to 62.9, with a spread of 36.3 and stddev of 7.29. The corpus also contains multiple conflicting SOTA claims, with different papers reporting best values such as 62.9 (InternImage-H), 61.5 (ViT-Adapter-L), and 59.9 (SwinV2-G).

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: The corpus does not show a direct disagreement about whether position embeddings are needed. The evidence consistently says vision transformers use positional information because self-attention is permutation-equivariant, e.g. one paper states, "positional encodings are commonly employed to incorporate the order of sequences" and that "Vision transformers follow the same fashion to imbue the network with positional information." Another paper frames its contribution as providing "the position information in vision transformers," which supports the same view rather than contradicting it.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy rose from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up from 2020 to a peak in 2022, followed by a downward trend through 2024.

questions.system_answer: The corpus has 49 papers in 2021, 21 in 2022, 12 in 2023, 7 in 2020, 5 in 2019, 4 in 2024, and 2 in 2025. The highest count is in 2021 with 49 papers.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: Average model parameter count increased substantially from 2020 to 2023, rising from ~161M in 2020 to ~4099M in 2023, before dropping in 2024 due to small sample size.
questions.system_answer: The average model parameter count rises sharply from 160.872M in 2020 to 290.974M in 2021, then to 780.582M in 2022, and peaks at 4,098.764M in 2023. It then drops steeply to 50.0M in 2024, so the overall trend is up through 2023 and down in 2024.
